abstract: Gaining a deeper mechanistic understanding of catalytic ammonia production from dinitrogen is essential for optimizing the synthesis of this critical molecule. In this study, we investigate the use of samarium diiodide (SmI₂) and water as a reductant/proton source in the presence of molybdenum complexes bearing PCP-type pincer ligands. Our kinetic investigation reveals that the rate-determining step shifts depending on catalyst loading: at high concentrations, the reaction is limited by the proton-coupled electron transfer (PCET) involved in N–H bond formation at the molybdenum imide center, whereas catalyst dimerization becomes the bottleneck at low concentrations. Guided by density functional theory (DFT) calculations, we designed a series of PCP-type pincer ligands featuring diverse substituents at the 5- and 6-positions. We found that incorporating electron-withdrawing groups significantly accelerates the reaction rate. Specifically, a molybdenum trichloride complex modified with trifluoromethyl groups emerged as the most potent catalyst, achieving a remarkable turnover number of 60,000 equivalents of ammonia and a turnover frequency of 800 equivalents per minute. These insights provide a robust framework for designing more efficient, environmentally sustainable nitrogen fixation systems.
